coredata.dc:description: Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) with amplified photoluminescence (PL) intensity were synthesized using recycled hot-water extraction wastes of lignocellulosic biomass according to a bio-refinery concept without carbonization of the entire biomass. The recycled waste liquid was obtained by pre-extracting biomass with pressurized hot-water under 120–180 °C for 30 and 60 min. CDs with controlled N-doping were obtained by a hydrothermal carbonization strategy of the pre-extraction liquid at 220 °C for 5 h. The main chemical component of pre-extraction hydrolysates was analyzed by high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC). The effects of hydrolyzed sugars and other heteroatom-containing extracts on fluorescence of CDs were investigated by measuring the PL performance. The superior biomass-based CDs exhibited tunable quantum yields (QYs) of 6.2 %–11.8 % with hot-water extractions as precursor without carbonization destruction of the entire biomass. CDs with higher QYs could serve as an effective luminescent sensing platform for probes, temperature sensing, and anti-counterfeit applications.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Distinct hot-water pre-extractions of biomass were recycled to synthesize CDs with tunable N/C ratio and amplified PL behavior by facile treatment without carbonization of the entire biomass.

(a) Mass loss of SG biomass after different hydrothermal conditions (insert: tendency of solid mass loss); (b) Mass fraction of hemicellulose and cellulose hydrolysates in the recycling extraction with respect to the whole hemicellulose and cellulose of biomass, respectively.

XRD patterns (a, c) and FTIR results (b, d) of extracted SG biomass and fluorescent CDs. The mark of “SG-120-60” refers to the residual SG solid biomass after extraction at 120 °C for 60 min, whereas the mark of “CDs-120-60-220” refers to CDs obtained using the recycling extraction (120 °C for 60 min) at 220 °C for 5 h.

Structural patterns of SG-derived CDs: TEM (a, d, g) and HRTEM (b, e, h) micrographs of CDs-120-60-220, CDs-150-60-220, and CDs-180-60-220 samples, respectively. Insets present the particle size distribution of CDs; Elemental distribution mapping of (c) C, (f) N, and (i) O for CDs-120-60-220 sample.

Chemical analysis and elemental contents of SG-produced CDs: (a, b, c) C1s results and fitting data of CDs-120-60-220, CDs-150-60-220, and CDs-180-60-220, respectively; (d, e, f) N1s spectra of CDs; (g) 13C NMR spectra of CDs with peak assignments; (h) Zeta potentials of different CDs solution; (i) Elemental contents of all CDs.

Thermal analysis of SG solid residuals. (a) TG and (b) DTG of residual biomass solid after different hot-water extractions at 120–220 °C; Color pattern of (c) wet and (d) oven-dried residual biomass after extraction treatment.

(a) PL emission of CDs (Ex = 370 nm); (b) UV–vis spectra of SG-derived CDs solution; (c) PL emission of CDs-120-60-220 solution (pH = 7) with excitation wavelengths at 300–520 nm; (d) Normalized PL spectra of CDs-120-60-220; PL emission spectra of CDs from (e) PW and YP (λex = 370 nm); (g) Decay lifetime of SG-derived CDs; (h) Dependence of PL intensity of CDs-120-60-220 solution on pH values (λex = 370 nm, λem = 456 nm); (i) TG analysis of SG-produced CDs.
Possible formation mechanism of SG-derived CDs from hot-water extractions.

Some potential applications for CDs: (a) PL emission of ONFC/CDs film at 243–383 K (△T = 20 K, Ex = 370 nm); (b) PL intensity ratio relationship with increasing temperature. Inset shows fitted curve; (c) Reversibility of ONFC/CDs film at 243–363 K; (d) Photographs of CDs solutions (0.5 mg/mL) under natural light (up) and 365 nm UV beam (down); (e) Applications of CDs in ONFC sensing film, logo, and anti-counterfeit printing ink; (f) Beans cultivated using H2O and CDs dispersion (2.0 mg/mL) from left to right.
Characterization of the main components in three different raw biomass feedstocks.

head.ce:title: Recycling hot-water extractions of lignocellulosic biomass in bio-refinery for synthesis of carbon nanoparticles with amplified luminescence and its application in temperature sensing

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The strategy of recycling hot-water pre-extraction wastes of lignocellulosic biomass was utilized to synthesize fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) with adjustable N-doping and amplified luminescence according to a bio-refinery concept without carbonization of the entire biomass, and the flexible film of high quality CDs with carboxylate nanofibrillated cellulose (ONFC) showed highly sensitive sensing to temperature of 243–343 K.

Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) with amplified photoluminescence (PL) intensity were synthesized using recycled hot-water extraction wastes of lignocellulosic biomass according to a bio-refinery concept without carbonization of the entire biomass. The recycled waste liquid was obtained by pre-extracting biomass with pressurized hot-water under 120–180 °C for 30 and 60 min. CDs with controlled N-doping were obtained by a hydrothermal carbonization strategy of the pre-extraction liquid at 220 °C for 5 h. The main chemical component of pre-extraction hydrolysates was analyzed by high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC). The effects of hydrolyzed sugars and other heteroatom-containing extracts on fluorescence of CDs were investigated by measuring the PL performance. The superior biomass-based CDs exhibited tunable quantum yields (QYs) of 6.2 %–11.8 % with hot-water extractions as precursor without carbonization destruction of the entire biomass. CDs with higher QYs could serve as an effective luminescent sensing platform for probes, temperature sensing, and anti-counterfeit applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent CDs have attracted increasing interest since their discovery due to their excellent PL performance, biocompatibility, and low toxicity (Souza et al., 2016). Recently, numerous reports were published on the preparation of CDs, such as peeling nanoparticles from graphene (Cao et al., 2012), thermal-treated ammonium citrate or other chemicals (Yang et al., 2014; Zhang et al., 2017), and the microwave pyrolysis of wool (Wang et al., 2016), fruit (Tyagi et al., 2016), etc. Compared to the synthesis techniques for CDs, there is a growing interest in utilizing wood and plant resources for CDs owing to their abundant carbohydrate and lignin. Meanwhile, hydrothermal carbonization of lignocellulosic biomass has proved to be a safe, non-toxic, and environmentally friendly method due to the accessibility of sustainable feedstock under industrial conditions. Compared to most wood materials, plants are more beneficial to synthesize fluorescent CDs due to its high content of non-structural extracts, which exhibits huge potential as the supplier of carbon (C) and heteroatoms (such as nitrogen (N)) to form good N-doped CDs. These functionalized CDs are widely used in imaging, photoelectric sensors, and ion detection, which significantly promote the hydrothermal treatment of biomass as a high-value platform (Liu et al., 2012).

Switchgrass (Panicum Virgatum L., denoted as SG) is a perennial energy crop native to most of eastern USA and is widely introduced in other countries with warm seasons (Ganjegunte et al., 2017). It gradually transforms into a dedicated candidate for bioenergy feedstock on an industrial scale owing to its fast-growing character, high biomass yield, and low cost (Chen et al., 2010; Lindsey et al., 2013). In recent studies for synthesis of CDs, the lignocellulosic materials were generally hydrothermally processed at 200–220 °C for an extended period of reaction time (Liang et al., 2014; Liu et al., 2014). However, dominant fluorescence properties of such CDs are relatively low without additional NH4OH or ethylenediamine passivation agents. The possible explanation mainly ascribed to different energy levels associated with various surface defects, which is caused by the relatively low heteroatom content or diverse surface functional groups (Jing et al., 2019; Liang et al., 2016). In hydrothermal biomass, it is likely that non-structural extracts, hemicellulose, as well as cellulose and lignin components, are all involved in the complicated chemical reaction in high temperature solutions. After obtaining a small amount of CDs (generally less than 1.0 wt% of biomass), most of the biomass (above 50 wt%) that has not completely hydrolyzed is carbonized at high temperatures up to 220 °C, thus resulting in a relatively low utilization of raw materials.

Hot-water extraction at relatively low temperature (120–180 °C) is an efficient pre-treatment strategy for treating lignocellulosic materials, which is widely applied in bio-refinery, pulping, and wood-plastic composites. Recently, some studies have focused on the extraction of hemicellulose sugars from biomass as part of bio-refinery process (Ghaffar et al., 2015). These pre-extraction liquids are refined to obtain hemicellulose and small molecule acids by a complicated purification process. However, the utilization of hemicellulose (typically 5-carbon sugars) is still the bottleneck of the current industry. Numerous studies are still inclined to remove hemicellulose, generally amounting to 25–35 wt% of feedstocks (Hosseinaei et al., 2011), as low-valued wastes compared to the utilization of cellulose and lignin (Chen et al., 2019; Kumagai et al., 2016; Zhou et al., 2019). Our group has studied the effect of pre-extraction on wood shavings and wood-plastic composites (Hosseinaei et al., 2012), which can effectively improve the mechanical behaviors of composites and decrease hygroscopicity due to the removal of extracts and hemicellulose. A large amount of sugars and non-structural extracts were contained in hot-water pre-extractions, which was most likely a useful and easily ignored material for preparing CDs. Additionally, this process can effectively avoid carbonization of the entire biomass under high temperatures (up to 220 °C). Compared to completely fractionate biomass into hemicellulose, cellulose, and lignin components, separating biomass into two major parts is helpful for energy conservation and broadening applications of biomaterials (Lu et al., 2016; Peng et al., 2018).

Herein, we report a facile strategy of recycling hot-water pre-extraction wastes of biomass as precursors of potential C and N sources to synthesize fluorescent CDs with adjustable N-doping and amplified luminescence according to a bio-refinery concept without carbonization of the entire biomass. The effects of different pre-extraction temperatures (120–180 °C) and time durations (30 and 60 min) were performed on extraction yields of de-branched sugars. Additionally, the differences for CDs derived from different hydrolysate and biomass (such as SG and wood) were also analyzed. To our knowledge, in comparison with the previously reported CDs synthesized via carbonization of biomass under high temperature (220 °C), this is the first reported method to synthesize high QY luminescent CDs from the pre-extraction wastes of lignocellulosic materials using bio-refinery concept without carbonization of the entire biomass. The superior applications of high QY CDs in temperature-dependent sensing film, anti-counterfeiting, and their negligible bio-toxicity in green beans model were also demonstrated.

The SG biomass including stem, leaves, and seeds was obtained from the main biomass supply platform in Knoxville (Tennessee, USA). The pine (Pinus Taeda L., denoted as PW) softwood and yellow poplar (Populus spp., denoted as PW) hardwood residues, mainly low-valued sawdust, were collected from the University of Tennessee. All biomass were naturally air-dried and hammer-milled through a 20-mesh sieve. Toluene (≥ 99.9 %), HCl (37 %), NaOH pellets, CaCO3 powder, and H2SO4 were all ACS grade and purchased from Fisher Scientific, USA. NaClO2 and acetic acid buffer (pH = 4.0) was purchased from RICCA company. Carboxylate nanofibrillated cellulose (ONFC) aqueous dispersion (0.3 wt%) with average charge density of 1.5 meq/g and carboxyl substituted ratio of 0.42 was obtained from Haojia company, Tianjin, China.

Biomass particles and deionized water (85.0 g) were blended in a beaker with a solid-to-liquid ratio of 1/17.5. These mixtures were then sealed in steel reactors (150 mL) with Teflon tube and placed in a fluidized sand-bath for hydrothermal treatment. The parameters of temperature were preheated as 120, 135, 150, 165, and 180 (at intervals of 15 °C) and a heating time of 30 min and 60 min, respectively. Subsequently, the wet mixtures were ultrasonically processed (200 W, 30 min) and filtered via a 0.22 μm film to achieve solid/liquid separation. The liquid was then collected as a pioneered precursor for synthesis of CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of fluorescent CDs from recycled pre-extraction wastes
ce:section.ce:para: Recycling pre-extraction solution (35.0 g) in 50 mL Teflon equipped autoclaves were sealed in an oven (220 °C for 5 h) to hydrothermal carbonization. The aqueous solution was then ultrasonic dispersed (200 W, 30 min) and centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 15 min. The yellowish liquid exhibited strong luminescence under a 365 nm UV beam. Furthermore, water dialysis (molecular-weight cutoff = 500) for 48 h was used to remove extra unreacted small molecules. The synthesis procedure of a series of CDs from water extractions is illustrated in Fig. 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of flexible ONFC/CDs fluorescent sensing film
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent CDs solution of 2 mL (0.5 mg/mL) was blended with ONFC aqueous suspension (60 g). The suspension was further stirred for 30 min with a magnetic rotor under ambient conditions to ensure uniform mixing between ONFC and fluorescent CDs. The mixture was then put into a dish with gentle shaking to ensure well-dispersed thickness of the suspension, and dehydrated at 40 °C with vacuum drying for 48 h to obtain a flat ONFC/CDs film. Finally, flexible sensing films with a wafer diameter of 100 mm (approximately 20–25 μm thick) were obtained.

The morphology of CDs was measured on a G2-F30 Transmission electron microscopy (FEI Tecnai, TEM) at 200 kV. Fluorescence spectra were conducted using Hitachi F-4500 (Tokyo, Japan) spectrophotometer and Jobin Yvon Fluorolog-3 modular fluorescence spectrometer (Horiba, France) with xenon lamp (450 W) as excitation source. Fluorescence decay lifetime was determined with a time-resolved systerm of the Jobin Yvon Fluorolog-3 spectrometer. The chemical compositions were performed using a Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectroscopy (Spectrum One, Perkin Elmer). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were obtained by a Thermo 250xi photoelectron spectroscopy analyzer. The UV–vis spectra were tested by an Evolution 60S UV–vis spectrophotometer (Fisher Scientific, USA). Elemental mapping was operated using a Quanta-200 scanning electron microscope (FEI Co., USA) coupled with an energy-dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) detector (10 kV) after same scanning time. Elemental composition was obtained using Elementar CHN analyzer (Vario EL cube). Zeta potential of suspensions was recorded by a Nano ZS zetasizer (Malvern, UK). Thermal gravimetric (TG) estimation was acquired on a Pyris-1 device (Perkin Elmer, USA) at a flow velocity of 20 °C/min under N2 atmosphere.

The carbohydrates and main degradations in extractions were tested based on TP-510-42623 standard. Briefly, sugar analysis was performed on a HPLC system (Model 2695, MA) equipped with a SP-G guard column and a SP-0810 analytical column. Analysis of the by-products of liquid was conducted using the same system with a Bio-Rad Aminex HPX-87H analytical column. All average values and standard deviations were based on three replicates.

The lignin, cellulose, and hemicellulose content were measured according to the modified Klason procedure (Kim et al., 2012). The non-structural components including H2O extracts, ethanol extracts, and ash were obtained according a solvent and TG analysis, respectively. The detailed parameters were provided in the supporting file.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) was evaluated using a XRD meter (Rigaku, Japan) under a fine-focus tube with voltage of 40 kV and 40 mA amperage. Diffraction intensity was measured at 10°–80° (4°/min). The crystallinity index (CrI) of the solid biomass after extraction was evaluated via the following Eq. (1) (Segal et al., 1959),
(1)
CrI (%) = (I200 – Iam)/ I200×100 %
where I200 is the maximum value at plane (200) (2θ = 22.4°), and Iam is the intensity between plane (200) and (110) (2θ = 18°) scattered by amorphous part of the biomass samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Main chemical composition analysis of solid biomass and hydrolysate liquid
ce:section.ce:para: The main components and relative element content of different raw biomass are presented in Table 1 . Three biomass species were all constituted with more than 80 % of structural components, such as cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin. However, it was obvious that SG biomass contained more non-structural components than that of YP and PW wood materials. Additionally, more N-containing compounds were detected for the SG biomass, which is more beneficial to synthesize high-quality fluorescent N-doped CDs due to its high content of heteroatoms.

The yield of dominating hydrolytic sugars and by-products of the SG extraction liquid under different processing conditions are given in Table 2 . At the lowest temperature of 120 °C, a small amount of cellobiose and glucose, as well as oligomeric sugars, were detected in the extraction after 60 min. This is mainly ascribed to the unique hydrolyzed sugars in non-structural components of SG and slight hydrolysis of the hemicellulose. This hydrolyzing trend remains gently increased from 120 °C to 135 °C. However, the total sugars increased significantly at 150 °C. This was mainly attributed to the glass transition of lignin (Rissanen et al., 2014), which affects the hydrothermal process and accelerates the hydrolysis of hemicellulose from biomass. Furthermore, the yield of cellobiose and glucose monomers, as well as glucan and xylan oligomers, reached the highest values at up to 165 °C. There was similar increasing trend in the presence of xylose oligomers up to 165 °C. Most of these oligomers further depolymerized to form xylose at 180 °C and 220 °C. As the temperature increases, both monomeric and oligomeric forms of glucose were degraded to form 5-hydroxymethyl furfural (5-HMF) and formic acid from 165 °C to 220 °C.

Moreover, there was an increase in the production of arabinan, galactan, and mannan sugars when temperatures reached higher than 180 °C, which indicates severe de-branching of hemicellulose from the raw biomass. As shown in Table 2, there was also an increase in acetic acid with the increasing temperature up to 180 °C. In addition, the hydrolyzed sugars were further depolymerized and degraded at higher temperature and longer time (220 °C for 3 h). It seems that a considerable amount of sugars remains in the hydrolysate at 220 °C for 3 h. For this reason, this processing condition (220 °C for 3 h) in the first report of grass-based CDs is not sufficient to hydrolyze and carbonize sugars to form enough fluorescent CDs (Liu et al., 2012). Meanwhile, it has been verified that longer reaction time was beneficial for the CDs formation from sugars, and the suitable thermal extraction time was 5–12 h (Liang et al., 2014; Liu et al., 2014). Accordingly, the CDs were synthesized at 220 °C for 5 h to further increase the carbonization yield from sugars to CDs.

Distinct pre-extraction processes (120–180 °C) were designed to compare with the hydrothermal carbonization of biomass at 220 °C. Fig. 2 a shows the mass loss of SG after different hydrothermal conditions. In the range of 120 °C–165 °C, the mass loss gradually increases as the temperature and time increase. In the following discussion, lignin content of biomass was regarded as constant after water extraction (120–180 °C for 60 min) due to its relatively high stability (Oliveira et al., 2013; Román et al., 2012). The increasing temperature (120–180 °C) mainly contributes to the removal of non-structural extracts and hemicellulose (Table 2). According to the (severity factor) S0 of reaction (Table S1), high S0 derived from temperature and time exacerbated this removal process. The extraction process significantly improved mass loss at 180 °C. The mass loss at 180 °C for 60 min extraction is as high as 32.6 % compared with that of 10.1 % at 120 °C. Meanwhile, the mass loss at 220 °C for 3 h and 5 h is 46.3 % and 48.6 %, respectively. There is no evident difference due to the high thermal stability of lignin and cellulose crystallized regions (Oliveira et al., 2013; Román et al., 2012), which also confirms longer carbonization time mainly accelerated hydrolysis of sugars and the formation of CDs. The pH values of extractions of SG are presented in Fig. S1. The results were consistent with the HPLC analysis owing to the generation of more acids with rising temperature.

The mass fraction of hemicellulose and cellulose hydrolysates in the recycling extractions is shown in Fig. 2b. The yield of hemicellulose sugars increased sharply with increasing S0, while a gentle downward tendency was detected for that of the cellulose hydrolysates. This is mainly due to the relatively high stability of cellulose compared to hemicellulose, which is more easily hydrolyzed to liquid during hydrothermal processes. The slight increase of cellulose hydrolysates in the initial stage (120–135 °C) was mainly ascribed to the non-structural sugars. As the temperature increases to 165 °C, cellulose sugars are partially degraded to form acetic and formic acids. Consequently, a large amount of 5-HMF and furfural was generated as temperature increases from 180 °C to 220 °C, which leads to the sharp decline of cellulose hydrolyzed sugars in the extractions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of extracted solid biomass and fluorescent CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To confirm that our pre-extraction method did not lead to carbonization of the solid biomass, XRD was employed to evaluate the crystalline behaviors of residual solid biomass after extraction (Fig. 3 a). Strong crystalline intensities corresponding to the (200) lattice plane at around 2θ = 22.4°, as well as diffraction peaks at 14.9° and 16.5° corresponding to the (110) and (1–10) lattice plane, were detected for all residual solid samples. Compared with 38.6 % for the raw SG, the relative crystallinity of residual solids gently increased from 40.4%–45.0% with an increasing temperature between 120–180 °C for the extracted biomass. This is mainly due to the removal of extracts and hemicellulose, which increases the relative intensity of cellulose. Meanwhile, the crystallinity of solid residue (220 °C for 5 h) is markedly increased to 56.8 %. The sharp peak at 22.4° also shows an enhanced carbon ordering degree. Meanwhile, a minor sharp diffraction peak appeared at 26.5°, which is analogous to the diffraction (002) peak of natural graphite, indicating that the raw material has partially undergone graphitization and carbonization after 5 h treatment at 220 °C.

The FTIR spectra of the biomass after extraction are shown in Fig. 3b. For the pre-extracted SG samples, there are no additional characteristic peaks compared with raw SG, except the increased shoulder intensity at 2845 cm−1. This is mainly due to the enhanced CH2 symmetrical stretching of cellulose and lignin with the increased removal of hemicellulose. However, hemicellulose proved to be absent due to the disappearance of 1736 cm−1 and 1262 cm−1 peaks for solid residues (220 °C, 5 h), which is attributed to the carbonyl and CO stretching of hemicellulose, respectively. The intensities at 1100 cm−1 and 898 cm−1, which are mainly related to holocellulose, decreased markedly after carbonization at 220 °C. In the meantime, the CO stretching at 1736 cm−1 was diverted to a lower wavelength (1690 cm−1), and the intensity was dramatically increased mainly due to the hydrolysis carbonization from lignin (Tjeerdsma and Militz, 2005). In addition, the intensity of aromatic skeleton vibrations at 1603 cm−1 and 1506 cm−1, derived from lignin, was markedly increased after carbonization. This is consistent with the previous results for hot-water pretreatment of wood stands (Hosseinaei et al., 2011). The intensity at 1163 cm−1 increased after carbonization, which implies the COC asymmetric stretching of holocellulose. This is probably due to the change in the structure of cellulose by reason of partial degradation in the amorphous zones (Roy et al., 1991).

XRD patterns of fluorescent CDs from SG with different synthesis conditions exhibit a typical broad peak at 21.6° (Fig. 3c). This is attributed to the amorphous carbon, which is consistent with that of previous biomass-based CDs (Zhao et al., 2015). FTIR results were further obtained to confirm the chemical constitution of CDs (Fig. 3d). The displayed bands of all CDs are basically the same. The peaks at 1710 cm−1 and 1605 cm−1 indicate the existence of CO and CC of C backbone, respectively (Zhao et al., 2015). The peak at 2900 cm-1 is the CH stretching and 1405 cm−1 is ascribed to the CN stretching vibrations. The symmetric vibration of COC peaks was detected at 1020 cm−1, while signal at 1124 cm−1 depicts the stretching vibration of CO bands in carboxyl groups. Meanwhile, it is apparent that the stretching vibration of OH at about 3302 cm−1 was markedly enhanced with increasing temperature, which probably ascertains the higher hygroscopicity of high temperature synthesized CDs.

Fig. 4 (a, d, g) show the TEM images of CDs obtained by process (220 °C for 5 h) of distinct recycling hot-water extractions from 120, 150, and 180 °C, respectively. These CDs are distributed among 2–8 nm and exhibited a similar particle size. The average particle sizes were 5.21 ± 0.54, 4.36 ± 0.62, and 3.52 ± 0.41 nm for CDs-120-60-220, CDs-150-60-220, and CDs-180-60-220, respectively. The particle sizes of CDs derived from treatment of lower temperature extraction (120 °C) were slightly larger than that of higher temperature (180 °C). This is probably ascribed to the integrated effects of sugars in extractions and pH condition, which affects the formation of CDs and subsequent particle distribution. The HRTEM images in Fig. 4(b, e, h) present that all CDs have a good crystalline C structure. A clear lattice fringe distance was observed at 0.21 nm, which probably ascribed to the sp2 (1120) crystalline phase of graphite (Alam et al., 2015). Fig. 4(c, f, i) show the EDS elemental mapping distributions for C, N, and O of CDs-120-60-220. The comparison for C and N elements of CDs-220−5 h are presented in Fig. S2. The presence of these elements indicates that the CDs were rich in C, N, and O elements.

XPS was further employed to determine the related chemical bonding states of CDs. Fig. S3 shows the full survey of XPS of CDs, which exhibits three distinct peaks centered at 285.0 eV (C1s), 399.6 eV (N1s), and 531.8 eV (O1s). High-resolution C1s XPS data of three kinds of CDs are illustrated in Fig. 5 (a–c). The C1s spectra can be converted into four component C peaks with binding energies near 284.4, 285.8, 286.8, and 288.5 eV, which suggests the containing of CC/CC, CO/CN, carbonyl, and carboxyl groups, respectively. This is in conformity to the functionalized CDs from onion carbohydrate (Bandi et al., 2016). These findings clearly reveal that the content of CO/CN ingredients of luminescent CDs significantly decreased with an increasing extraction temperature from 120 °C to 180 °C. Meanwhile, the content of COOH groups increased slightly.

The N1s spectra in Fig. 5(d–f) can be properly fitted into three component traits at 399.2, 400.6, and 401.8 eV, which are ascribed to the pyridine N, graphitic N, and NH groups, respectively. With increasing temperature from 120 °C to 180 °C, the intensity of graphitic N gradually improves, whereas that of the amino N centered at 401.8 eV declines. In addition, the peak of amino N slightly shifts to a higher binding energy with descending N content. This feature is in line with previous luminescent nanoparticles obtained with different N contents (Yu et al., 2018). To further understand the surface functional groups on CDs, the 13C NMR spectra and Zeta potentials were obtained, as shown in Fig. 5(g, h). The 13C NMR peaks for different CDs keep high resemblance. The 13C NMR of CDs revealed strong O-bonded aliphatic compounds (72.5 ppm), methenyl (61.8 ppm), and aliphatic groups (20.0–35.4 ppm). Moreover, the CDs-220−5 h had stronger intensity around 180.2 and 129.8 ppm, which represents the carboxyl/amide and aromatic C, respectively. Nonetheless, the structure for SG-produced CDs is different from the traditional grapheme CDs, which exhibits strong carbon-aromatic peaks near 129.8 ppm (Suryawanshi et al., 2014). The slight aromatic structure for CDs-120-60-220, CDs-150-60-220, and CDs-180-60-220 mainly contributed to polymerization of non-structural aromatic compounds with hydrolyzed sugars. For the 13C NMR of CDs-220−5 h, the increasing intensity of aromatic C skeletons probably ascribed to partial degraded lignin in severe hydrothermal reaction. The Zeta potential for CDs was measured as a negative charge. The absolute Zeta potential value of CDs-220−5 h (−14.4 mV) was obviously higher than that of the CDs from lower temperature extractions. It mainly ascribed to the increasing acid and furfural content in the hydrolysate and further affects the acetylation degree of sugars, which is in conformity to the HPLC and XPS data. Fig. 5(i) lists the relative element content of CDs. The N content in CDs was significantly decreased from 6.75 % to 3.83 % with increasing temperature at 120–180 °C, and further declined for that of CDs-220−5 h. The ratio of C to N changes from 6.79, 8.29, to 10.9 for CDs-120-60-220, CDs-150-60-220, and CDs-180-60-220, respectively. This indicates the decreasing N content in pre-extractions with increasing temperature. This is consistent with the HPLC result of more sugars in the extraction with increasing temperature (120–180 °C), thereby reduced the relative content of element N to C in the final CDs.

TG and DTG analysis were performed to illustrate the effect of pre-extraction (120–180 °C) on the thermal behavior of biomass compared with the severe treatment under 220 °C. Fig. 6 a shows the TG data for residual biomass solid after extraction at 120–220 °C. As shown in Fig. 6a, the mass loss rate of SG-220−5 h displays an obvious difference between control and pre-extracted biomass. For SG-220−5 h, the residues exhibit a lower weight loss compared with those of the pre-extracted samples under 120–180 °C. This was mainly ascribed to the partial carbonization of biomass at 220 °C, which is consistent with the XRD results.

The DTG of SG-220−5 h also show the lowest thermal degradation peak at 388 °C compared with the control (362 °C) and other samples (Fig. 6b). In the case of pre-treated samples at 120–180 °C, the degradation trend shows no big difference except the weakened shoulder peak near 300 °C with increasing temperature. However, the residual solid of SG-180-60 still contained slight hemicellulose due to the occurrence of DTG shoulder peak (Fig. 6b) at 305 °C (Na et al., 2014). Fig. 6(c, d) present the color pattern of wet and dry residual SG biomass after extraction. Colors of the control and pre-treatment residuals were similar and entirely different from SG-220−5 h, indicating that this pre-extraction method was effective in avoiding severe carbonation of biomass materials.

Fig. 7 a shows the PL emission spectra of CDs from recycling hot-water extraction liquid of SG and comparative spectra with CDs from other wood substrates. The CDs liquids were adjusted to the same solid-to-liquid ratio (0.5 mg/mL) and pH condition (pH = 7.0) to compare the PL behavior. The PL intensity of CDs obtained from pre-extraction of SG was markedly higher than that of wood feedstock (Fig. 7a). This was mainly because SG has more extracts and higher N/C ratio than the wood substrate (Table 1). This result is consistent with previous reports on N-doped CDs (Liang et al., 2014), which exhibits better PL behavior than those of absolute CO skeleton. Better PL intensity of CDs is exhibited for lower pre-extracted temperature. This was mainly because partial N-doping extracts are relatively easy to be delaminated into extraction. Accordingly, high quality CDs were prepared through controlling the hemicellulose amount in the pre-extraction liquid, thus obtained a series of CDs with tunable N/C ratio and amplified PL behavior. The PL emission peak of CDs-220−5 h from SG (Fig. 7a), YP, and PW (Fig. 7(e, f)) exhibits a slight redshift compared to that of CDs prepared from low-temperature pre-extractions. This result is probably due to PL overlapping effect of sugar-based CDs and few lignin degradations (220 °C for 5 h), because the PL peak (Ex = 370 nm) of aromatic lignin is located at 460–475 nm (Chen et al., 2016).

As shown in UV–vis absorption spectra in Fig. 7b, all CDs have a strong absorption at about 280 nm, which is assigned to the π–π* electron transition. This feature is in conformity to previous reports of CDs (Amjadi et al., 2016; Sahu et al., 2012; Shi et al., 2016; Thambiraj and Shankaran, 2016). The highest QY for the SG-derived CDs were calculated as 11.8 % (CDs-120-60-220), which is much higher than that of CDs-220−5 h of 5.58 % (Table S2). Furthermore, the QY progressively declined from 11.8 % to 6.7 % for the CDs synthesized from recycling pre-extractions at 120–180 °C. These results indicated that controlling the hydrolyzed degree of SG is a feasible and effective way to improve the hot-water pre-extractions and obtain a series of CDs with tunable PL intensity. Additionally, CDs prepared via hydrothermal carbonization from wood materials showed lower QY of 2.4 % and 3.2 % for YP and PW, respectively. Noticeably, the mechanism for synthesis of CDs from recycling hot-water extractions of biomass involving extracts release, sugar hydrolysis, carbonization and recombination is illustrated in Fig. 8 .

Fig. 7c shows the PL spectra of CDs-120-60-220 dispersion with λex ranging in 300–520 nm. The PL intensity significantly increases from 300 nm to 360 nm and gradually decreases with increasing λex up to 520 nm. The maximum PL emission behavior was appeared at λex of 360 nm. Specifically, the normalized PL emission spectra of CDs dispersion depicted multicolor behavior tuned from 452 nm (violet) to 556 nm (yellowish-green), with an increasing λex at 360–520 nm (Fig. 7d). Such observation is consistent with other biomass-based CDs (Qin et al., 2013; Tyagi et al., 2016). Numerous researches have suggested that this tunable emission behavior is mainly attributed to the different surface energy traps and/or quantum effects of CDs (Baker and Baker, 2010; Wang et al., 2009). Furthermore, the PL stability of CDs-120-60-220 solution on pH values was demonstrated to evaluate their applicability in practical applications (Fig. 7h). The PL emission behavior of CDs solution (monitored at 456 nm) fluctuated gently as the pH values varied at 5.6–9.5, and declined dramatically in the pH range of 1.8–5.6 and 9.5–13.4. Therefore, the optimized CDs environment needs to be 6.0–8.5 to obtain better PL behavior.

The PL decay lifetime (monitored at 450 nm with 370 nm excitation) was further obtained to analyze the potential difference among the SG-based CDs (Fig. 7g). The decay curves were fitted well with a second-order exponential model (Fig. S4). The average lifetime of CDs synthesized using pre-extractions was higher than that of CDs-220−5 h (4.05 ns). Additionally, the lifetime of CDs was gently improved with a reduced extraction temperature from 180 °C to 120 °C. The maximal lifetime (6.03 ns) was detected for the precursor of minimum temperature of pre-extraction (120 °C). This feature is in keeping with the PL intensity and probably ascribed to the effect of higher N/C ratio. The thermal analysis of CDs was detected by TG in N2 up to 940 °C (Fig. 7i). CDs present quicker thermal degradation compared with SG biomass (Fig. 6a). This result is attributed to the quick decomposition of hydrophilic groups of CDs (Sachdev and Gopinath, 2015). However, the degradation behavior of CDs-220−5 h was slower than that of CDs from pre-extractions. In addition, the CDs from pre-extractions (120–180 °C) present more rapid weight loss. The final residual weight for CDs-120-60-220, CDs-150-60-220, CDs-180-60-220, and CDs-220−5 h was 5.15 %, 12.0 %, 22.1 %, and 24.7 %, respectively. It is mainly ascribed to the effect of more sugar-induced skeletons in CDs with increasing extraction temperature, which leads to improved thermal stability and formation of higher weight of residues.

The possible formation mechanism of SG-derived CDs from water extractions can be explained as follows. In the initiation stage (120–180 °C), the C6 sugars of glucan, galactan, and mannan, as well as the C5 sugars of xylan and arabinan were inclined to decomposition and further produced some soluble furfural like compounds of 5-HMF, and furfural, respectively. In the meantime, several acid by-products of acetic and formic acids were generated. As the carbonization process (220 °C for 5 h) further extended, the polymerization of 5-HMF, non-structural active extracts (such as lutein, flavonoids, chlorophyll, and soluble proteins, etc.), and furfural compounds probably occurred under such weak acid conditions. The ratio of non-structural extracts and sugars in the recycling extractions declined with the rising temperature at 120–180 °C, thereby affecting the polymerization. The active polymers in mixture were condensed and recombined to form partial aromatic clusters of CDs. Consequently, it is a feasible strategy to synthesize fluorescent CDs through controlling the hydrolyzed degree of SG biomass, thus obtained good CDs from the recycling extractions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications of high-quality CDs in fluorescence sensing
ce:section.ce:para: To illustrate the potential usability of CDs as a fluorescent indicator in practical applications, some flexible films were developed using ONFC as skeleton and CDs as functional filler to perform their temperature-sensing behavior. One of the main functions of ONFC in this film was its good dispersion behavior and restraint of the PL extinction defect owing to the non-dispersion of CDs (Fig. S5), thereby promoted PL stability of CDs signal (Lin et al., 2019; You et al., 2016). Fig. 9 a shows the PL spectra of ONFC/ CDs-120-60-220 film in range of 243–383 K under 370 nm irritations. The PL intensity progressively declined as the temperature increased from 243 K to 383 K (Fig. 9a). This is derived from the heat-induced quenching behavior of most CDs with increasing temperature (Cui et al., 2017). Additionally, CDs signals displayed a spot of red-shift with rising temperatures. This is mainly derived from the acceleration of non-radiant tracks of surface trap (or defect) states, thereby resulting in decreased PL behaviors and the slight red-shift effect (Eda et al., 2010).

The relative ratio between the PL intensity of a certain temperature (FT ) and room temperature (F0 ) at 450 nm are also presented (Fig. 9b). A good linearity was observed for the values of FT (450)/ F0 (450) within the temperature range of 243–343 K. The linearity can be described as follow Eq. (2),
(2) T = 505.89 – 207.90 FT (450)/ F0 (450)
where FT (450) and F0 (450) are the corresponding PL emission intensity of a certain temperature (FT ) and room temperature (F0 ) at 450 nm, respectively. T is the detected temperature for the ONFC/CDs sensing film. The obtained correlation coefficient was 0.984.

Furthermore, the thermal response of ONFC/CDs sensing film was reversible upon temperature cycling in 243–343 K (Fig. 9c). The good cycling behavior also suggests that ONFC/CDs films have promising applications in temperature sensing. However, it showed non-reversible for the film when temperature increased to 363 K. This can be ascribed to the unrecoverable PL quenching behaviors because of partial damage of the surface functional groups of CDs during heating and cooling cycles. Accordingly, the suitable temperature for the ONFC/CDs sensing film should be limited at 243–343 K to obtain good reusable behavior for temperature monitoring.

Photographs of CDs solutions from processing pre-extraction wastes of SG displayed higher PL intensity than those of the one-step hydrothermal process (Fig. 9d). The CDs solutions directly synthesized from SG and wood materials (HY and PW) under 220 °C showed lower PL intensity. This is in accordance with the PL behavior of CDs suspensions in Fig. 7a. Moreover, the morphology and hygroscopicity of the solid-state CDs were presented in Fig. S6. All the CDs showed stronger hygroscopicity and aggregated to chunks upon exposure to moist air. This may be explained via the rising apparent charge density of particles with carbonization and aromatization of hydrolyzed sugars. Some applications of CDs with high QY, such as ONFC/CDs sensing film, label, and anti-counterfeit printing ink are demonstrated in Fig. 9e. A toxicity study of CDs was performed using plants in accordance with a previous method (Xu et al., 2014). The growth experiments of green beans were introduced to visually evaluate the bio-toxicity of CDs solution. Fig. 9f presents the growing morphology of green beans cultivated with CDs solution compared with water after 5 days of growth. Both groups of bean sprouts grew well and displayed no big difference. However, the bean sprouts grown in CDs solution present strong PL emission under 365 nm UV beam. This is because the CDs nanoparticles easily entered the cell cavity of bean sprouts due to the small size of CDs. In contrast, bean sprouts exhibit biological pathology when beans are cultivated with metallized CdTe CDs solution (Xu et al., 2014). Consequently, the CDs synthesized using hot-water extractions of SG biomass are of nontoxicity (or low toxic) with respect to the growth of green beans and are thus expected to be applied to broader researches of plants or other living cells.

In summary, hot-water pre-extraction wastes of biomass were recycled to synthesize fluorescent CDs with adjustable N-doping, QYs, and amplified luminescence according to a bio-refinery concept without carbonization of the entire biomass. The yields of extracts and de-branched sugars in hot-water extractions can be adjusted with increasing temperature between 120–180 °C, thereby leading to distinct N-doping CDs after high temperature carbonization strategy (220 °C for 5 h). Compared with previously reported CDs synthesized through one-step hydrothermal biomass under 220 °C, our method not only achieved a series of CDs with strong PL emission and high QYs (up to 11.8 %), but also effectively avoided carbonization of the entire biomass without affecting post- pulping and other high-value utilization. The blue-green CDs exhibited tunable QYs (6.2 %–11.8 %) with the pre-extraction wastes (120–180 °C) of SG biomass as precursor. We have also demonstrated the superior applications of high QY CDs in fluorescence temperature sensing films, anti-counterfeit printing ink, and their negligible bio-toxicity in plant growth model. Overall, this method is expected to provide a practical high-valued utilization platform for the industrialized recycling hot-water pre-extraction waste liquids of biomass.

Siqun wang and Longfei Zhang mainly provided the concept and designed the experiments including method and solving strategies. Shaoyi Lyu and Qijun Zhang assisted in completing the XPS and FTIR analysis. Stephen C. Chmely provided lots of help in coordinating the device of sand bath and discussing some key questions in hydrothermal process. Yuntao Wu and Chuck Melcher performed large amount of PL tests. Kalavathy Rajan and David P. Harper achieved the HPLC tests and precise analysis. Longfei Zhang carried out the validation of repetitive experiments and finished the whole text writing. Siqun wang and Zhilin Chen mainly revised the manuscript. All the authors read and approved the final manuscript.
